title: Heaps
description: Heaps power top-K, median-finding, and priority queue problems. Since TypeScript has no built-in heap, you must implement a min-heap manually — interviewers expect you to know the array-based representation and sift operations.

sections.title: Manual min-heap implementation
sections.explanation: A min-heap is a complete binary tree stored in an array where each parent is smaller than its children. For index `i`, the left child is `2*i + 1`, right child is `2*i + 2`, and parent is `Math.floor((i - 1) / 2)`. You need `push` (add + bubble up) and `pop` (remove min + bubble down). Interviewers expect you to write this from scratch in TypeScript.

exercise.prompt: Complete the MinHeap class with push (bubble up) and pop (bubble down). Push values 10, 4, 15, 1, 7. Print three consecutive pops.

validation.message: Implement MinHeap with bubbleUp and bubbleDown methods

exercise.hint: Parent index is Math.floor((i - 1) / 2). Left child is 2*i + 1, right child is 2*i + 2.

sections.title: Top-K elements with heap
sections.explanation: The top-K pattern uses a min-heap of size K. For each element, push it onto the heap. If the heap exceeds size K, pop the minimum. After processing all elements, the heap contains the K largest. This runs in O(n log K) — much better than sorting O(n log n) when K is small.

exercise.prompt: Using the MinHeap class, complete the topK function that finds the K largest elements. Print the result for nums = [7, 10, 4, 3, 20, 15] with k = 3.

validation.message: Use min-heap of size k to find top-K elements

exercise.hint: When heap size exceeds k, pop the minimum. This keeps only the k largest values in the heap.

sections.title: K-th smallest element
sections.explanation: To find the K-th smallest, use a max-heap of size K — but since we only have a min-heap, we can negate values to simulate a max-heap. Push negated values; when size exceeds K, pop (removes the largest negative, i.e., the largest original). The top of the heap (negated back) is the K-th smallest.

exercise.prompt: Complete the kthSmallest function using negation to simulate a max-heap. Find the 3rd smallest element in [8, 1, 6, 3, 9, 2]. Print the result.

validation.message: Negate values to simulate a max-heap using a min-heap

exercise.hint: Push -n to simulate a max-heap. The peek gives the most negative value (largest original). Negate it back with -(maxHeap.peek()!).

sections.title: Merge K sorted arrays
sections.explanation: Merging K sorted arrays is a classic heap problem. Push the first element of each array into a min-heap (with its array and element index). Pop the minimum, add it to the result, then push the next element from that same array. This produces a fully sorted result in O(N log K) where N is total elements.

exercise.prompt: Complete the mergeKSorted function. Push [value, arrayIndex, elementIndex] tuples into the heap. Print the merged result for [[1,5,9],[2,6],[3,4,7,8]].

validation.message: All elements merged and sorted: 1 through 9
Use heap to track next element from each sorted array

exercise.hint: Initial element index is 0. Check if elemIdx + 1 < arrays[arrIdx].length before pushing the next element. Advance elemIdx by 1.
